persons.arguments_against: The strongest case against: the biography carries effectively a single initial ('Albert' -> 'A.'), and the 1921 staff-list row prints 'A. A. Abramson' with TWO initials, suggesting a different person (e.g. a relative such as a son or brother also serving in Palestine), with the deterministic pass failing on colony, tenure, position, honours, and edition co-occurrence. Why it fails: the mechanical failures are an artifact of the dossier's place-inheritance, which wrongly tagged the post-1921 bio events as Transjordan; the verbatim bio text shows these are Palestine-administration posts. The careers then match clause-for-clause across four distinct, specific offices with consistent staff-list lag: bio 'ohmn., land coman., and dir., land dept' 1920 vs staff list 'Chairman' of 'Land Commission' 1921; bio 'dist. gov., southern dist' 1922 vs 'District Governor' 1923-24; bio 'dist. comanr., northern dist' 1925 vs 'District Commissioner' 1927-28; bio 'comanr., lands, 1927' vs 'Commissioner of Lands' 1929-32. A hypothetical second A. Abramson would have to hold this exact four-office sequence on the same dates, in a corpus where only 1 official and 1 biography bear this surname. The stray second initial appears in exactly one OCR'd row (1921) and reverts to 'A. Abramson' in all eight later years, so an OCR/typesetting artifact is far likelier than a distinct person. Hebron (1918, military governorate) and Transjordan (1921 chief British representative) were both administered under the Palestine mandate establishment, consistent with the Palestine staff list.

evidence_cited.fact: ohmn., land coman., and dir., land dept., 1920

persons.would_overturn: Evidence of a second contemporaneous A. Abramson in the Palestine administration (e.g. a staff list or biography distinguishing 'A. A. Abramson' from 'Albert Abramson' in the same edition, or a bio for an 'A. A. Abramson' with a different career), or a primary source showing Albert Abramson held the 1920s land/district posts somewhere other than Palestine.

notes: The deterministic FAILs (colony, tenure_overlap, position_sim, edition_cooccurrence) all stem from the bio timeline's '(inherited from previous clause)' Transjordan tagging; the printed entry itself describes Palestine-mandate offices that track the Palestine staff-list rows one-for-one with the usual 1-2 year publication lag. Single-initial trap considered and discharged by four independent specific position+date matches on a surname unique in the corpus (1 official, 1 biography). The lone 'A. A. Abramson' double-initial row (1921) is treated as an OCR/printing artifact since all other years print 'A. Abramson'.
